---
title: "1.2 Variables and Data Types"
subtitle: "Part 2: Declaring, Assigning, and Naming Variables"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Declaring Variables in Java

### Type and name

To create a variable, you must tell Java its data type and its name.

Creating a variable is also called <span class="term">declaring a variable</span> (声明变量).

The type is a keyword like `int`, `double`, or `boolean`, but you get to make up the name for the variable.

When you create a primitive variable, Java will set aside enough bits in memory for that primitive type and associate that memory location with the name that you used.

## Bits and Memory

### Type determines storage

Computers store all values using <span class="term">bits</span> (binary digits).

A bit can represent two values, usually `0` or `1`.

When you declare a variable, Java needs to know how many bits to use and how to represent the value.

::: {.tight-list}
- An integer gets <span class="mark">32 bits</span> of memory.
- A double gets <span class="mark">64 bits</span> of memory.
- A boolean could be represented by just one bit.
:::

## Memory Figure

### Types use different memory space

::: {.image-fit}
![Examples of variables with names and values showing different memory space](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-2-variables-and-data-types/typesAndSpace.png){fig-align="center" width="72%"}
:::

Different types get a different amount of memory space.

## Declaration Syntax

### Type, name, semicolon

To declare a variable, specify the type, leave at least one space, then the name for the variable and end the line with a semicolon `;`.

```java
int score;
```

Java uses the keyword `int` for integer, `double` for a floating point number, and `boolean` for a Boolean value.

## Assigning a Value

### `=` sets the value

After declaring a variable, you can give it a value using an equals sign `=` followed by the value.

```java
int score;
score = 4;
```

Or you can set an initial value in the variable declaration:

```java
int score = 4;
```

## Printing Variables

### Concatenation and quotes

When printing variables, you can use the string concatenation operator `+` to add them to another string inside `System.out.print`.

Never put variables inside quotes `""` because that will print out the variable name letter by letter.

You do not want to print out the variable name, but the value of the variable in memory.

If you want spaces between words and variables, you must put the space in the quotes.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: strConcatDemo`

Textbook prompt: Run the following code to see what is printed.

Then, change the values and run it again.

Try adding quotes to variables and removing spaces in the print statements to see what happens.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: strConcatDemo`

```java
public class Test2
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int score;
        score = 0;
        System.out.print("The score is ");
        System.out.println(score);

        double price = 23.25;
        System.out.println("The price is " + price);

        boolean won = false;
        System.out.println(won);
        won = true;
        System.out.println(won);

        String name = "Jose";
        System.out.println("Hi " + name);
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: strConcatDemo`

Runestone expects this starter output from `main`:

```text
The score is 0
The price is 23.25
false
true
Hi Jose
```

## Identify Declarations

### `clickablearea:: var_declare`

Textbook prompt: Click on all of the variable declarations in the following code.

```java
public class Test2
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int numLives;
        numLives = 0;
        System.out.println(numLives);
        double health;
        health = 8.5;
        System.out.println(health);
        boolean powerUp;
        powerUp = true;
        System.out.println(powerUp);
    }
}
```

## Correct Lines

### `clickablearea:: var_declare`

Variable declarations start with a type and then a name.

```java
int numLives;
double health;
boolean powerUp;
```

## Identify Initializations

### `clickablearea:: var_init`

Textbook prompt: Click on all of the variable initializations, the first time the variable is set to a value.

```java
public class Test2
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int numLives;
        numLives = 0;
        System.out.println(numLives);
        double health = 8.5;
        System.out.println(health);
        boolean powerUp = true;
        System.out.println(powerUp);
    }
}
```

## Correct Lines

### `clickablearea:: var_init`

Variables are initialized using `name = value;`.

```java
numLives = 0;
double health = 8.5;
boolean powerUp = true;
```

## Assignment Direction

### Variable on the left

The equal sign here `=` does not mean the same as it does in a mathematical equation.

Here it means set the value in the memory location associated with the variable name on the left to a copy of the value on the right.

A variable always has to be on the left side of the `=`, and a value or expression on the right.

## Debugging Task

### `activecode:: asgn_order`

Textbook prompt: This assignment statement is in the wrong order. Try to fix it to compile and run.

```java
public class Test3
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int score;
        4 = score;
        System.out.println(score);
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: asgn_order`

Runestone expects this output from `main`:

```text
4
```

The target assignment has the variable on the left and the value on the right.

## Trace Checkpoint

### `fillintheblank:: fillDecVar1`

Fill in the following:

```java
[blank] age = [blank];
```

Prompt: declare `age` to be an integer and set its value to `5`.

Correct response:

```java
int age = 5;
```

## Trace Checkpoint

### `fillintheblank:: fillDecVar2`, `fillDecVar3`

What type should you use for a shoe size like `8.5`?

Correct response:

```text
double
```

What type should you use for a number of tickets?

Correct response:

```text
int
```

## Mixed-Up Code

### `parsonsprob:: declareVars1`

Textbook prompt: Declare and initialize variables for a number of visits, a person's temperature, and if the person has insurance or not.

Needed blocks:

```java
int numVisits = 5;
double temp = 101.2;
boolean hasInsurance = false;
```

## Extra Blocks to Reject

### `parsonsprob:: declareVars1`

```java
Int numVisits = 5;
Double temp = 101.2;
Boolean hasInsurance = false;
```

Reject these blocks because Java primitive type keywords are lowercase.

## Naming Variables

### Rules

While you can name your variable almost anything, there are some rules.

::: {.tight-list}
- A variable name should start with an alphabetic character.
- It can include letters, numbers, and underscores `_`.
- It must be all one word with no spaces.
- You cannot use keywords or reserved words as variable names.
:::

Examples of keywords include `for`, `if`, `class`, `static`, `int`, and `double`.

## Naming Variables

### Meaningful names and camel case

The name of the variable should describe the data it holds.

A name like `score` helps make your code easier to read. A name like `x` is not a good variable name in programming because it gives no clues about what kind of data it holds.

The convention in Java is to start a variable name with a lower case letter and uppercase the first letter of each additional word, for example `gameScore`.

This is called <span class="term">camel case</span> (驼峰命名).

## Naming Note

### Source rules

::: {.tight-list}
- Use meaningful variable names.
- Start variable names with a lower case letter and use camelCase.
- Variable names are case-sensitive and spelling sensitive.
- Each use of the variable in the code must match the variable name in the declaration exactly.
- Never put variables inside quotes `" "`.
:::

## Debugging Task

### `activecode:: varCaseSensitive`

Textbook prompt: Java is case sensitive, so `gameScore` and `gamescore` are not the same.

Run and fix the code below to use the right variable name.

```java
public class CaseSensitiveClass
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int gameScore = 0; // variable name using camel case
        // this is using a different variable without camel case!
        System.out.println("gameScore is " + gamescore);
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: varCaseSensitive`

Runestone expects this output from `main`:

```text
gameScore is 0
```

The variable name must match the declaration exactly.

## Trace Checkpoint

### `fillintheblank:: fillName1`, `fillName2`

What is the camel case variable name for a variable that represents a shoe size?

```text
shoeSize
```

What is the camel case variable name for a variable that represents the top score?

```text
topScore
```

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- declare a variable using type, name, and semicolon
- explain why Java needs the data type before reserving memory
- initialize a variable with its first value
- print a variable value without putting the variable name in quotes
- put the variable on the left side of `=`
- use meaningful camelCase names with exact capitalization
:::

## End

### Continue to Part 3

[Next: Weather and Mad Libs challenges →](1-2-part-3-weather-and-mad-libs-challenges.html)
